result:
:::writing
There’s a particular hour of the night that doesn’t belong to anyone. It’s not quite late, not quite early, a seam in time where the day has loosened its grip but morning hasn’t yet found the thread to stitch things back together. In that hour, everything feels slightly misplaced, like a chair pulled an inch too far from the table.

I like that hour. It makes room for thoughts that don’t fit anywhere else.

The city outside my window hums in a softer register then. Cars pass, but they’re not in a hurry. Somewhere, a dog barks once and then decides against making a second statement. A single light burns in an apartment across the street, and I imagine someone pacing, or reading, or staring at nothing in particular. There’s comfort in that anonymous companionship—the idea that other people are awake for reasons they may not fully understand.

I used to think clarity came in the morning. There’s a mythology around it, isn’t there? Wake up early, greet the sun, organize your thoughts before the world interrupts. But clarity, I’ve found, is a shy animal. It doesn’t appear when summoned. It wanders in when you’re not paying attention, when you’ve stopped trying to force things into order.

Tonight, for example, I sat down intending to write something precise. Something with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Something that would justify the time spent producing it. Instead, I’ve found myself circling a feeling I can’t quite name.

It has something to do with distance.

Not physical distance, though that’s part of it. The miles between places, the hours between time zones, the way a familiar street can become strange if you haven’t walked it in a while. But it’s more than that. It’s the distance between who you thought you would be and who you are, between what you meant to say and what actually came out, between the person someone remembers and the person you’ve quietly become.

That distance is rarely dramatic. It doesn’t announce itself. It accumulates in small increments, like dust on a shelf you forgot you owned. One day you notice it—not because something has changed, but because you finally paused long enough to look.

I think about the people I’ve known, the ones who drifted out of orbit without any formal goodbye. There’s no single moment where they disappeared. Instead, there were postponed plans, messages left unanswered a little longer each time, until eventually silence became the default language. It’s tempting to assign blame, to trace the exact point where things could have gone differently, but that’s a kind of fiction. Most endings aren’t events. They’re processes.

And yet, despite that, I carry these people with me. Not in a heavy, burdensome way, but like echoes. Certain phrases, certain jokes, certain ways of seeing the world—they linger. Sometimes I catch myself thinking a thought that doesn’t quite feel like mine, and I realize it’s something I absorbed from someone who hasn’t been in my life for years.

It’s strange, how we’re made of each other like that.

The clock on the wall ticks with unnecessary enthusiasm, as if it’s proud of its consistency. I wonder who first decided that time should be divided so neatly, that seconds should march in lockstep, that minutes should stack into hours with such rigid predictability. It’s useful, of course. Without it, everything would dissolve into approximation. But there’s something artificial about it, too. My experience of time doesn’t feel like a sequence of identical units. It stretches, contracts, loops back on itself.

A single afternoon can feel endless if you’re waiting for something. A year can collapse into a blur if nothing distinct marks its passage. And then there are moments—brief, almost insignificant at the time—that expand in memory until they occupy entire landscapes in your mind.

I remember standing in line at a grocery store years ago, watching the person in front of me carefully count out coins. There was nothing remarkable about it, no dramatic context, no life-altering consequence. And yet, for some reason, that moment has stayed with me. The quiet concentration, the small clink of metal, the patience of the cashier—it all felt complete in a way that’s hard to describe.

Maybe that’s what I’m circling around tonight. The idea that meaning isn’t always tied to magnitude. That a life isn’t defined solely by its milestones, but by the accumulation of small, self-contained moments that don’t seem important until they are.

There’s a tendency to treat life like a narrative that needs to be optimized. To look for arcs, for progress, for evidence that things are moving toward something better. And while that’s not entirely wrong, it can be limiting. It turns every experience into a means to an end, every present moment into a stepping stone rather than a place to stand.

What if some moments are enough on their own?

The light in the apartment across the street has gone out. Whoever was awake has surrendered to sleep, or at least to the attempt. The street is quieter now, the hum reduced to something closer to a whisper. This is the part of the night where it feels like the world has exhaled and forgotten to inhale again.

I realize I haven’t solved anything. Whatever I sat down to understand remains just out of reach. But the feeling has shifted, softened at the edges. It’s less like a problem now and more like a companion—something that can sit beside me without demanding to be resolved.

There’s a kind of peace in that.

Not everything needs to be clarified, categorized, concluded. Some things can remain open, unfinished, in motion. Like this hour of the night, which will eventually give way to morning without ever having fully explained itself.

The sky is beginning to lighten, almost imperceptibly. A faint suggestion of blue where there was only black before. Soon, the city will wake up in earnest. The cars will return with purpose, the sidewalks will fill, the anonymous companionship of sleepless strangers will dissolve into the structured interactions of the day.

But for now, there’s still this in-between space. This quiet, unclaimed territory where thoughts can wander without needing to arrive anywhere.

I think I’ll stay here a little longer.
:::writing

The cars will return with purpose, the sidewalks will fill, the anonymous companionship of sleepless strangers will dissolve into the structured interactions of the day.

:::writing
